Scenario.title: Juicy Mix Attack on Water/Wastewater Infrastructure
Scenario.overview: Water and Wastewater Systems Complex A sophisticated multi-stage attack targeting industrial control systems (ICS) in water/wastewater facilities, leveraging zero-day vulnerabilities, social engineering, and lateral movement to disrupt critical operations.
Scenario.attack_surface: AVAILABLE_SURFACES: Target System Artifact, Script, Internet-facing assets, OS Process, Network Traffic, File System, Application Log, Target Artifact (Privileges), Target Artifact (Stored)
Scenario.attack_vector: AVAILABLE_VECTORS: Open-Source Intelligence (OSINT) Gathering, Script Execution (Mechanism), Delivery Method, Exploit TEE Vulnerability, Stored Data Manipulation, Application Log Content (Mechanism), Process Injection, Mimikatz Tool, Network Traffic Content (Mechanism), Pass-the-Hash Over Industrial Networks, File Access (Mechanism), Network Connection Creation (Mechanism), Runtime Data Manipulation

stage.name: Reconnaissance
stage.description: The attacker collects publicly available information about the water/wastewater facility's infrastructure, vendor ecosystems, and network architecture to identify potential entry points and vulnerabilities.

stage.name: Resource Development
stage.description: The threat actor develops custom scripts targeting ICS/SCADA protocols to craft payloads capable of exploiting vulnerabilities in legacy industrial systems.

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: The attacker exploits a vulnerability in Langflow's API to deliver stripped payloads via spear-phishing emails, bypassing authentication mechanisms to compromise endpoints.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC-67: String Format Overflow in syslog()
stage.description: Malicious code is executed via unpatched ICS software, leveraging memory corruption flaws to escalate privileges and establish a foothold in the network.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: The attacker modifies system schedules to persist malicious processes, ensuring continued access even after rebooting affected devices.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Default/admin credentials in ICS systems are exploited by manipulating application logs to gain elevated privileges across the OT network.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.description: Malicious code is hidden within legitimate system processes to avoid detection by security tools and maintain stealth.

stage.name: Credential Access
stage.capec: CAPEC-226: Session Credential Falsification through Manipulation
stage.description: Authentication credentials are extracted from memory using Mimikatz to access restricted system components and propagate laterally.

stage.name: Discovery
stage.description: Network traffic analysis identifies critical assets like pumps, sensors, and control systems within the OT network to prioritize targets.

stage.name: Lateral Movement
stage.description: Stolen credentials are used to move laterally between devices without triggering traditional endpoint defenses, expanding the attack surface.

stage.name: Collection
stage.description: Operational data is collected from local systems to identify targets for disruption, including process statuses and configuration files.

stage.name: Exfiltration
stage.description: Stolen data is exfiltrated using fragmented UDP packets to evade detection while maintaining low-profile communication over public networks.

stage.name: Impact
stage.capec: CAPEC-230: Serialized Data with Nested Payloads
stage.description: Fake commands are injected into running processes to alter water flow rates or disable critical equipment, causing operational disruptions.

Scenario.summary: A complex, multi-phase attack leveraging OSINT, zero-day exploits, and lateral movement to compromise water/wastewater infrastructure, culminating in operational sabotage through runtime data manipulation.
